attributes.stem: A six-year-old girl presents to the General Practice clinic with her parent concerned about dark spots on her neck that have been present for two years. She has asthma and coeliac disease, and takes fluticasone and salbutamol. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple dark, plugged, pore-like lesions arranged in a linear pattern along the left side of her neck. The lesions are asymptomatic and do not express any contents when squeezed. There are no other similar lesions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of comedo naevus (naevus comedonicus), which is characterised by grouped, comedone-like lesions arranged in a linear pattern following Blaschko's lines. The rare condition is typically present at birth or develops in early childhood, and the lesions are fixed and unchanging. Multiple similar lesions together with neurological, skeletal and ophthalmological abnormalities may indicate naevus comedonicus syndrome however a singular comedo naevus is benign and not associated with other abnormalities.

Comedo naevus is a type of epidermal naevus or hamartoma that represents a developmental abnormality of the pilosebaceous unit or hair follicles. Unlike true comedones, these lesions cannot be expressed and do not respond to typical acne treatments of tetracyclines or benzyl peroxide, but can be managed if required with tretinoin or salicylic acid.

Acne vulgaris typically presents with a mixture of comedones, inflammatory papules, and pustules. The lesions would not follow a linear pattern and would not have been present since early childhood in a child this young unless there is virilisation which would then have other associated symptoms such as body odour, breast development or abnormal growth. Acne vulgaris typically occurs during puberty on the face and may involve the shoulders.

Dermatofibroma presents as firm, brown or skin-coloured nodules that typically appear after minor trauma. They are usually solitary lesions and demonstrate the 'dimple sign' when compressed laterally rather than dark lesions.

Keratosis pilaris presents as small, rough follicular papules typically on the upper arms and thighs. While they can be numerous, they do not follow a linear pattern and do not resemble comedones.

Acne neonatorum is a self-limiting condition that occurs in newborns due to maternal androgens. It presents with inflammatory papules and pustules on the face and resolves spontaneously within weeks to months of birth up to 1 year.
learningPoints: Comedo naevus (epidermal naevus) typically presents as grouped comedone-like lesions in a linear pattern following Blaschko's lines.
The condition is usually present at birth or develops in early childhood.
references: Sivasaththive M, Keefe M, Mitchell G. Comedo naevus. DermNet NZ. 2022. Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/comedo-naevus
Yadav P, Mendiratta V, Rana S, Chander R. Nevus Comedonicus Syndrome. Indian J Dermatol. 2015. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4533567/
